content.full_text:
Farn Polrrtcal PnecrICES CouvttssloN
428 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
Jarruary 6,2004
Michael J. Mais
City of Long Beach
Office of the City Attorney
333 West Ocean Boulevard, Eleventh Floor
Long Beach, CA 90802-4664
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4.-03-302
Dear Mr. Mais:
This letter is in response to your request for reconsideration, on behalf of Long
Beach Airport Advisory Commissioner Don Temple, of advice issued previously in the
Temple Advice Letter, No. 4-03-262,regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTION
Does Commissioner Don Temple have a conflict of interest in an upcoming
discussion and vote relating to an environmental impact report covering proposed
construction projects at the Long Beach Airport?
CONCLUSION
Under the facts as we understand them, the effects of this govemmental decision
on Mr. Temple's economic interests are presumed not to be material and, so long as this
presumption is not rebutted by specific facts to the contrary, Mr. Temple will not have a
conflict of interest in the upcoming discussion and vote.
' Gorernm"rt Code sections 81000 - g1}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
lq1,!


File No. A-03-302
Page No. 2
FACTS
In the Temple Advice Letter, No. 4-03-262, we determined that real property
owned by Mr. Temple was directly involved in decisions regarding proposed construction
projects at an airport whose outer property boundaries are situated within 500 feet of the
parcels owned by Mr. Temple. Because this property was directly involved in these
decisions (under Commission regulations), we concluded from the facts before us that it
would be reasonably foreseeable that these decisions would have some financial effect on
Mr. Temple's economic interest in his real property, andthat in such a case Mr. Temple
would be disqualified from participating in those decisions.
In more recent communications you provided materials showing that the location
of Mr. Temple's real property is approximately 3,300 feet from the actual site of the
proposed airport terminal enhancements. The point you emphasize in these materials is
the limitation of the construction project to a discrete and confined area within the 1,116
acre site that constitutes the airport property. In particular, you note that the proposed
terminal improvements are designed to be consistent with the "noise budget" and other
flight stipulations already in effect at the airport. You indicate that the proposed project
and its effects will therefore be limited to a well-defined areaimmediately surrounding
the existing terminal and its appurtenances, a portion of the airport property more than a
half-mile distant from the properties owned by Mr. Temple.2
ANALYSIS
The facts you present in your request for firrther consideration have no bearing on
the initial steps of our analysis in the prior letter, and do not alter our conclusions that
Mr. Temple is a public official who anticipates making or participating in the making of
governmental decisions, and that he has economic interests in two parcels of real property
situated within 500 feet of the airport property lines, and in two business entities operated
from those properties. The above-referenced facts do, however, affect our conclusion in
step four of the conflict of interest analysis, whether Mr. Temple's economic interests are
directly involved in the decisions at issue. We begin our analysis from that point.
Under regulation 1ffi0a.2@)(1), real property in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest,
or any part of that real property, is located within 500 feet
of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the governmental decision.
For purposes of subdivision (aX5), real property is located
2 You have also advised us that there is no legal basis for Mr. Temple's understanding that each airport
commissioner has advisory responsibilities to a particular "assigned" council member, in addition to the
obligation of the airport commissioners as a body to advise the city council as a body. It is your under-
standing that Mr. Temple has not had any "business-related" communications with the council member to
whom he was unofficially "assigned" by the commission chairman as an informational resource.


File No. A-O3-302
Page No. 3
'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries)
of the real property which is the subject of the govern-
mental decision' if any part of the real property is within
500 feet ofthe boundaries (or proposed boundaries) ofthe
redevelopment proj ect area."
In our prior letter we determined from the plain language of this regulation that
both of Mr. Temple's real property interests were "directly involved" in decisions on the
airport terminal improvement project. The Commission has, however, crafted an
exception to the literal boundaryto-boundary rule, applicable in cases where the
governmental decision affects only a clearly defined, specific and isolated site, such as a
particular building on a large tract of land. (See, e.g. the Witson Advice Letter, No. A-
03-007.. the Ball Advice Letter, No. A-01-279; the Kaplan Advice Letter, No. A-98-224.)
The Kaplanletter is particularly instructive here. The subject of one of the
decisions reviewed in that letter was the lease of a particular parcel of airport property
Because it seemed that this decision might well affect the number of aircraft using the
airport, we could not conclude that the effects of that decision would be confined to a
remote and isolatedpart of the airport property.
The facts you have provided us indicate that the circumstances are different in this
case. As described, the anticipated improvernents to the terminal area are not designed to
accommodate different patterns of air or ground traffic likely to affect areas at a distance
from the terminal and their immediate surroundings. [n this circumstance, we conclude
that Mr. Temple's property, a half-mile from the actual site of the project, is not directly
involved in decisions relative to this project.3
When real property is not directly involved in a govemmental decision, financial
effects on that property are presumed not to be material. (Regulation 18705.2(b).) This
presumption is not absolute, however, and wil be rebutted if there are specific circum-
stances regarding the decision, its financial effect, or the nature of the official's real
property interest that make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on that real property. (Ibid.) We have no information on such "specific
circumstances," and must leave it to you or Mr. Temple to determine whether there are
circumstances warranting rebuttal of the presumption.a
Assuming that there are no facts or circumstances rebutting the presumption that
effects of the decision on Mr. Temple's real property will notbe material, and assuming
3 As stated in our previous letter to Mr. Temple, his economic interests in the two business entities are also
not directly involved in these decisions. Our analysis as to these two economic interests remains the same
as set forth earlier, and we will not consider them further in this letter, which will focus on modifications to
the analysis caused by our revised conclusion that Mr. Temple's real property interests are not directly
involved in the decision at issue.
a We note that the discussion and decision in question concerns an environmental impact report for the
project. Mr. Temple should be able to learn from the EIR whether there are "specific circumstances"
rebutting a presumption that the decision will have no material financial effect on his economic interests.


File No. A-03-302
Page No. 4
the same regarding effects on his interests in the two business entities operating from
these properties, Mr. Temple will not have a conflict of interest in a decision regarding
the airport terminal improvement project.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
/
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
LTwjg
